ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese curry rice combo with beef, chicken karaage, and egg. FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (curry, prepared dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Japanese curry rice combo with multiple protein components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for Japanese curry rice combo. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not extend to this level of specificity for composite Japanese dishes.

serving.description: 1 combo plate with rice, beef curry, chicken karaage, half-boiled egg, and miso soup
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 650 g, comprising a bowl of Japanese curry rice with beef, two to three pieces of chicken karaage, one half-boiled egg, and a bowl of miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Japanese Curry Rice with Beef and Chicken — composite estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Curry sauce (prepared), White rice (cooked), Beef (cooked, braised), Chicken (fried, batter-coated), Egg (hard-boiled), Miso soup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Japanese curry rice combo. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for: cooked white rice (~45% by weight), Japanese-style curry sauce with beef (~30% by weight), deep-fried chicken karaage (~15% by weight), half-boiled egg (~7% by weight), and miso soup (~3% by weight). Per-100g values are derived from this composite. Values are cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Curry, Japanese style' (FDC ID 174278) where available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice (cooked, Japanese short-grain) and curry-based mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific combo. GI of 60 is estimated as a weighted composite: Japanese short-grain cooked white rice has a GI of approximately 64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); Japanese curry sauce with beef lowers the effective GI due to fat, protein, and fibre content of the sauce. The composite GI of 60 is consistent with published values for Japanese curry rice dishes (medium GI range). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 120.25) / 100 ≈ 72.2, rounded to 72.5. Serving carbohydrate = 18.5 g per 100g × 650g serving = 120.25g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry sauce, miso soup, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of this combo provides approximately 2015 mg of sodium (310 mg per 100g × 650g), which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium curry sauce, and avoid the miso soup to reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large portion of white rice in curry sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 72 per serving and a medium GI of 60, this combo causes a substantial postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to half, choose brown rice if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef, deep-fried chicken karaage, and curry roux; high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from beef and deep-fried chicken karaage (approximately 11.7 g saturated fat per serving) and high sodium content makes this meal a moderate-to-high cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, remove the karaage skin, and opt for a smaller rice portion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deluxe Curry Rice Combo (デラックスカレーライスセット, Derakkusu Karē Raisu Setto) is a Japanese restaurant combo meal consisting of Japanese-style curry rice topped with braised or simmered beef, accompanied by chicken karaage (Japanese deep-fried chicken), a half-boiled egg (ajitsuke tamago style), and a bowl of miso soup or a drink. The curry sauce is a thick, mildly spiced Japanese roux-based sauce — sweeter and less pungent than South Asian curries — served over a generous mound of steamed Japanese short-grain rice. The chicken karaage is marinated in soy sauce, ginger, and garlic before being coated in potato starch and deep-fried to a crispy golden exterior with a juicy interior. The half-boiled egg contributes a creamy yolk and additional protein. This combo is a popular comfort food in Japanese casual dining chains such as Sukiya and is widely available in Singapore through food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (approximately 962 kcal per serving), high in carbohydrates from the rice, moderate in protein from beef, chicken, and egg, and high in sodium primarily from the curry sauce and miso soup. The glycemic load is high due to the large rice portion, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish contains gluten (wheat in curry roux and karaage coating), egg, soy, and dairy allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite Japanese curry rice combo with beef, chicken karaage, and egg. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for Japanese short-grain rice and curry-based mixed dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in the component USDA SR Legacy entries for this composite. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used for this estimation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry moderate uncertainty. The serving size of 650g is an estimate based on typical Japanese curry rice combo portions at casual dining chains; actual restaurant portions may vary. The miso soup component contributes significantly to the sodium total; if the drink option is chosen instead of miso soup, sodium per serving would be approximately 1700 mg. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific combo was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (60 × 120.25) / 100 = 72.15, rounded to 72.5.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Removed menu-style numbering '971.' from food_name to produce canonical English name 'Deluxe Curry Rice Combo'.
Iteration 1: Added Japanese local name デラックスカレーライスセット and romanization Derakkusu Karē Raisu Setto to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Japanese cuisine.
Iteration 1: Constructed weighted-component nutrient estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for all major components (rice, curry sauce with beef, chicken karaage, egg, miso soup).
Iteration 1: Set GI to 60 (medium) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for Japanese short-grain rice and curry-based dishes; documented estimation basis in gi_source.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes field to satisfy G3.
Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency — all Japanese script strings (デラックスカレーライスセット, カレーライスセット, カレーライス) and romanized strings confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases list.
Iteration 2: Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed values ('exact', 'closest_parent', 'none') — confirmed compliant.
Iteration 2: Verified no zero-macro justification rule triggered — all core macros are non-zero, rule not applicable.
